root.label: Cell Morphology
root.description: After confirming Gram positive stain, determine the shape of the cells under the microscope.
root.question: What is the shape of your Gram positive organism?

catalase_Gpos_coccus.label: Catalase Test
catalase_Gpos_coccus.description: Add a drop of 3% hydrogen peroxide to a colony. Bubbling (O₂ release) = positive. Catalase breaks down H₂O₂ into water and oxygen.
catalase_Gpos_coccus.question: What is the result of the catalase test?

novobiocin.label: Novobiocin Sensitivity
novobiocin.description: Your lab manual has an exercise (#35) dedicated to how to test for antibiotic sensitivity. We can test novobiocin along with other antibiotics using this test. Dr. Hussa will decide which other antibiotics to test (based on what we have in stock)
novobiocin.question: What is the result of the novobiocin sensitivity test?

result_epidermidis.label: Staphylococcus epidermidis
result_epidermidis.description: Staphylococcus epidermidis is Gram positive, catalase positive, mannitol negative, not yellow, and novobiocin sensitive.

result_saprophyticus.label: Staphylococcus saprophyticus
result_saprophyticus.description: Staphylococcus saprophyticus is Gram positive, catalase positive, mannitol negative, not yellow, and novobiocin resistant.

hemolysis.label: Hemolysis on Blood Agar
hemolysis.description: Observe colony growth on sheep blood agar. Alpha (α) = partial/green hemolysis. Beta (β) = complete clear hemolysis. Gamma (γ) = no hemolysis.
hemolysis.question: What type of hemolysis is observed on blood agar?

optochin.label: Optochin Sensitivity
optochin.description: Place an optochin (P) disk on the plate. Zone of inhibition ≥ 14 mm = sensitive. Optochin differentiates S. pneumoniae from viridans streptococci.
optochin.question: What is the result of the optochin sensitivity test?

result_pneumoniae.label: Streptococcus pneumoniae
result_pneumoniae.description: Gram positive, catalase negative, alpha hemolytic, optochin sensitive. Leading cause of community-acquired pneumonia, meningitis, and otitis media. Colonies are characteristically lancet-shaped diplococci.

result_viridans.label: Viridans Streptococci
result_viridans.description: Gram positive, catalase negative, alpha hemolytic, optochin resistant. A diverse group of streptococci; normal oral flora. Can cause subacute bacterial endocarditis in susceptible patients.

bacitracin.label: Bacitracin Sensitivity
bacitracin.description: Place a bacitracin (A) disk on the plate. Any zone of inhibition = sensitive. Differentiates Group A from Group B Streptococcus.
bacitracin.question: What is the result of the bacitracin sensitivity test?

result_pyogenes.label: Streptococcus pyogenes
result_pyogenes.description: Gram positive, catalase negative, beta hemolytic, bacitracin sensitive. Group A Streptococcus. Causes strep throat, impetigo, scarlet fever, and rheumatic fever. PYR positive.

result_agalactiae.label: Streptococcus agalactiae
result_agalactiae.description: Gram positive, catalase negative, beta hemolytic, bacitracin resistant. Group B Streptococcus. Important cause of neonatal meningitis and sepsis; screened in pregnant women.

bile_esculin.label: Bile Esculin + 6.5% NaCl Tolerance
bile_esculin.description: Bile esculin agar: darkening = positive. 6.5% NaCl broth: turbidity = growth. Enterococci are bile esculin positive AND salt tolerant.
bile_esculin.question: Are both bile esculin AND 6.5% NaCl tests positive?

result_enterococcus.label: Enterococcus spp.
result_enterococcus.description: Gram positive, catalase negative, gamma hemolytic, bile esculin positive, salt tolerant. Common in the GI tract; causes UTIs and endocarditis. Notorious for antibiotic resistance (VRE).

result_strep_group_d.label: Non-enterococcal Group D Streptococcus
result_strep_group_d.description: Gram positive, catalase negative, gamma hemolytic, bile esculin positive but NOT salt tolerant (6.5% NaCl negative). Includes S. bovis / S. gallolyticus. Associated with colorectal carcinoma.

spore.label: Endospore Stain
spore.description: Find the endospore stain procedure in the lab manual. We will use the microwave proceedure.
spore.question: Are endospores present in the rod-shaped organism?

oxygen.label: Oxygen Requirements
oxygen.description: If an organism is strictly anaerobic, it will not grow in the presence of oxygen.
oxygen.question: What are the oxygen requirements of the spore-forming rod? You do not need to do an experiment for this...

result_clostridium.label: Clostridium spp.
result_clostridium.description: Gram positive, endospore-forming, anaerobic rod. Includes C. difficile (antibiotic-associated colitis), C. botulinum (botulism), C. tetani (tetanus), and C. perfringens (gas gangrene).

acid_fast.label: Acid Fast Stain
acid_fast.description: Find the acid-fast staining procedure in the lab manual.
acid_fast.question: Is the organism acid-fast?

catalase_rod.label: Catalase Test
catalase_rod.description: Add a drop of 3% hydrogen peroxide to a colony. Bubbling (O₂ release) = positive. Catalase breaks down H₂O₂ into water and oxygen.
catalase_rod.question: Is the organism catalase positive or negative?

mycobacterium.label: Mycobacterium smegmatis
mycobacterium.description: Mycobacterium smegmatis: Gram positive rods, endospore negative, acid fast.

glucose_fermentation_gpos_rods.label: Glucose fermentation Test
glucose_fermentation_gpos_rods.description: The glucose fermentation test is PART of a series of tests labeled "Carbohydrates I: Fermentation and beta galactosidase activity" in you manual. We will use the Durham tube procedure.
glucose_fermentation_gpos_rods.question: is your organism positive for glucose fermentation?

lactobacillus_fermenti.label: Lactobacillus fermenti
lactobacillus_fermenti.description: Lactobacillus fermenti is gram positive, negative for endospores, negative for acid fast staining, negative for catalase activity, and positive for Glucose fermentation, producing acid and gas

Mannitol_fermentation_test.label: mannitol fermentation test
Mannitol_fermentation_test.description: The mannitol fermentation test is described within the "streak plate technique" test section of your lab manual (exercise 15).
Mannitol_fermentation_test.question: Does your organism ferment mannitol?

Lactobacillus_casei.label: Lactobacillus casei
Lactobacillus_casei.description: Lactobacillus casei is a gram positive rod that is negative for spores, negative for acid fast stain, negative for catalase activity, produces acid only from glucose fermentation, and is positive for mannitol fermentation

Lactobacillus_delbrueckii.label: Lactobacillus delbrueckii
Lactobacillus_delbrueckii.description: Lactobacillus delbrueckii is a gram positive rod that is negative for spore formation, negative for acid fast stain, negative for catalase activity, produces acid only from glucose fermentation, and is negative for mannitol fermentation

starch_hydrolysis.label: starch hydrolysis test
starch_hydrolysis.description: Find the starch hydrolysis experiment in your lab manual.
starch_hydrolysis.question: Does your organism use amylase to hydrolyze starch?

Corynebacterium_kutsceri.label: Corynebacterium kutsceri
Corynebacterium_kutsceri.description: Corynebacterium kutsceri is a gram positive rod that is negative for spore formation, negative for acid fast staining, positive for catalase activity, and positive for starch hydrolysis

Corynebacterium_xerosis.label: Corynebacterium xerosis
Corynebacterium_xerosis.description: Corynebacterium xerosis is a gram positive rod that is negative for spore formation, negative for acid-fast staining, positive for catalase activity, and negative for starch hydrolysis

Bacillus_Starch.label: Starch Hydrolysis
Bacillus_Starch.description: Find the starch hydrolysis experiment in your lab manual.
Bacillus_Starch.question: Does your organism use amylase to hydrolyze starch?

Bacillus_Voges_Proskauer.label: VP test (Voges-Proskauer) Bacillus
Bacillus_Voges_Proskauer.description: Find the Voges-Proskauer test in your lab manual. Note that this is a part of a series of tests in the exercise, and the first part of the procedure is listed under "methyl red test," because the methyl red and voges-proskauer tests are often done together as the "MR-VP test"
Bacillus_Voges_Proskauer.question: Does your organism produce 2,3 butanediol as a result of glucose fermentation?

bacillus_catalase.label: Catalase Test (bacillus)
bacillus_catalase.description: Add a drop of 3% hydrogen peroxide to a colony. Bubbling (O₂ release) = positive. Catalase breaks down H₂O₂ into water and oxygen.
bacillus_catalase.question: What is the result of the catalase test?

Swollen_cell_bacillus.label: Swollen Cell (amylase negative)
Swollen_cell_bacillus.description: No test is needed for this. Were your cells swollen (bulging) around the spore during sporulation (endospore test)? Ask Dr. Hussa for clarification.
Swollen_cell_bacillus.question: Was your sporulating cell swollen?

citrate_amylase_negative.label: Citrate Test (amylase negative)
citrate_amylase_negative.description: The citrate test is part of a group of tests called IMViC, described in exercise 23 of your book. Please read through the procedure to find the materials required for the citrate test.
citrate_amylase_negative.question: Can your bacterium use citrate as a sole carbon source?

Bacillus_azotoformans.label: Bacillus azotoformans
Bacillus_azotoformans.description: Bacillus azotoformans is a gram positive rod that makes endospores and grows under aerobic conditions. It is amylase negative, catalase negative, and citrate positive.

b_larvae_or_popilliae.label: Bacillus larvae or Bacullus popilliae
b_larvae_or_popilliae.description: we cannot easily distinguish between these two in our lab (talk to Dr. Hussa)

are_you_sure.label: are you sure?
are_you_sure.description: If you have reached this page, please consult with Dr. Hussa on how to complete your analysis

Cell_Diameter_bacillus.label: Cell Diameter (Bacillus)
Cell_Diameter_bacillus.description: This test does not require a separate experiment. Consult with Dr. Hussa to determine the answer to this question
Cell_Diameter_bacillus.question: Is your organism greater than 1 micrometer in width (diameter)?

citrate_test_bacillus_amylase_positive.label: Citrate test (amylase positive)
citrate_test_bacillus_amylase_positive.description: The citrate test is part of a group of tests called IMViC, described in exercise 23 of your book. Please read through the procedure to find the materials required for the citrate test.
citrate_test_bacillus_amylase_positive.question: Can your bacterium use Citrate as the sole carbon source?

Mannitol_Fermentation_amylase_positive.label: Mannitol Fermentation (Amylase Positive)
Mannitol_Fermentation_amylase_positive.description: The mannitol fermentation test is described within the "streak plate technique" test section of your lab manual (exercise 15).
Mannitol_Fermentation_amylase_positive.question: Does your organism ferment Mannitol?

Bacillus_Polymyxa.label: Bacillus polymyxa
Bacillus_Polymyxa.description: Bacillus polymyxa is a gram positive rod that makes endospores and grows under aerobic conditions. It is amylase positive, vogues proskauer positive, <1 micrometer, citrate negative, and mannitol positive.

Bacillus_alvei.label: Bacillus alvei
Bacillus_alvei.description: Bacillus alvei is a gram positive rod that makes endospores and grows under aerobic conditions. It is amylase positive, vogues proskauer positive, <1 micrometer, citrate negative, and mannitol negative.

6_5_percent_NaCl_growth.label: 6.5% NaCl growth
6_5_percent_NaCl_growth.description: Ask Dr. Hussa. This isn't in your lab manual. Basically, we need to have growth media that is comprised of 6.5% NaCl. Regular growth medium has 1% NaCl.
6_5_percent_NaCl_growth.question: Can your organism grow in 6.5% salt medium?

Bacillus_coagulans.label: Bacillus coagulans
Bacillus_coagulans.description: Bacillus coagulans is a gram positive rod that makes endospores and grows under aerobic conditions. It is amylase positive, vogues proskauer positive, <1 micrometer, citrate positive, and cannot grow in 6.5% salt (NaCl).

Growth_at_55_degrees_celsius.label: Growth at 55 degrees celsius
Growth_at_55_degrees_celsius.description: This is not in your lab manual. We instead just need to try and grow your organism in regular growth medium at 55 degrees celsius, then observe whether the organism grew successfully.
Growth_at_55_degrees_celsius.question: Can your organism grow at 55 degrees?

Bacillus_subtilis.label: Bacillus subtilis
Bacillus_subtilis.description: Bacillus subtilis is a gram positive rod that makes endospores and grows under aerobic conditions. It is amylase positive, vogues proskauer positive, <1 micrometer, citrate positive, can grow in 6.5% NaCl, and cannot grow at 55 degrees.

Bacillus_licheniformis.label: Bacillus licheniformis
Bacillus_licheniformis.description: Bacillus licheniformis is a gram positive rod that makes endospores and grows under aerobic conditions. It is amylase positive, vogues proskauer positive, <1 micrometer, citrate positive, can grow in 6.5% NaCl, and can grow at 55 degrees.

swollen_cell_amylase_positive.label: swollen cell (amylase positive)
swollen_cell_amylase_positive.description: No test is needed for this. Were your cells swollen (bulging) around the spore during sporulation (endospore test)? Ask Dr. Hussa for clarification.
swollen_cell_amylase_positive.question: Was your sporulating cell swollen?

citrate_test_amylase_pos_vp_neg.label: citrate test (amylase positive VP negative)
citrate_test_amylase_pos_vp_neg.description: The citrate test is part of a group of tests called IMViC, described in exercise 23 of your book. Please read through the procedure to find the materials required for the citrate test.
citrate_test_amylase_pos_vp_neg.question: Can your bacterium use Citrate as the sole carbon source?

Bacillus_megaterium.label: Bacillus megaterium
Bacillus_megaterium.description: Bacillus megaterium is a gram positive rod that makes endospores and grows under aerobic conditions. It is amylase positive, vogues proskauer negative, does not swell during sporulation, and is citrate positive.

Bacillus_badius.label: Bacillus badius
Bacillus_badius.description: Bacillus megaterium is a gram positive rod that makes endospores and grows under aerobic conditions. It is amylase positive, vogues proskauer negative, does not swell during sporulation, and is citrate negative.

mannitol_fermentation_g_pos_coccus.label: mannitol fermentation (Gram positive coccus)
mannitol_fermentation_g_pos_coccus.description: The mannitol fermentation test is described within the "streak plate technique" test section of your lab manual (exercise 15).
mannitol_fermentation_g_pos_coccus.question: Does your organism ferment mannitol?

Staph_aureus.label: Staphylococcus aureus
Staph_aureus.description: Staphylococcus aureus is a gram positive coccus shaped organism that is catalase positive and can ferment mannitol.

yellow_colony.label: yellow pigmentation
yellow_colony.description: This does not require an experiment. Look at the colonies of your unknown organism. They may be obviously yellow (not beige).
yellow_colony.question: Are your colonies yellow?

glucose_fermentation_gpos_coccus.label: Glucose fermentation (gram + coccus)
glucose_fermentation_gpos_coccus.description: The glucose fermentation test is PART of a series of tests labeled "Carbohydrates I: Fermentation and beta galactosidase activity" in you manual. We will use the Durham tube procedure.
glucose_fermentation_gpos_coccus.question: Does your organism ferment glucose?

micrococcus_varians.label: Micrococcus varians
micrococcus_varians.description: Micrococcus varians is a Gram positive coccus shaped organism that is catalase positive, cannot ferment mannitol, does produce a yellow pigment, and can ferment glucose.

Micrococcus_luteus.label: Micrococcus luteus
Micrococcus_luteus.description: Micrococcus luteus is a Gram positive coccus shaped organism that is catalase positive, cannot ferment mannitol, does produce a yellow pigment, and cannot ferment glucose.
